coredata.dc:title: A two-step strategy for simultaneous dual-mode detection of methyl-paraoxon and Ni (Ⅱ)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Mai
Chen, Ling
Wei, Jinchao
Cui, Xiping
Cheng, Zehua
Wang, Ting
Chao, Incheng
Zhao, Yunyang
Gao, Hao
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Exogenous pollution of Chinese medicinal materials by pesticide residues and heavy metal ions has attracted great attention. Relying on the rapid development of nanotechnology and multidisciplinary fields, fluorescent techniques have been widely applied in contaminant detection and pollution monitoring due to their advantages of simple preparation, low cost, high throughput and others. Most importantly, synchronous detection of multi-targets has always been pursued as one of the major goals in the design of fluorescent probes. Herein, we firstly develop a simultaneous sensing method for methyl-paraoxon (MP) and Nickel ion (Ni, Ⅱ) by using carbon based fluorescent nanocomposite with ratiometric signal readout and nanozyme. Notably, the designed system showed excellent effectiveness even when the two pollutants co-exist. Under the optimum conditions, this method provides low limits of detection of 1.25 µM for methyl-paraoxon and 0.01 µM for Ni (Ⅱ). To further verify the reliability, recovery studies of these two analytes were performed on ginseng radix et rhizoma, nelumbinis semen, and water samples. In addition, smartphone-based visual analysis has been introduced to expand its applicability in point of care detection. This work not only expands the application of the dual-mode approach to pollutant detection, but also provides insights into the analysis of multiple pollutants in a single assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methyl-paraoxon
Nickel ion
Dual-mode
Ratiometric detection
Visual analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The procedure of real sample analysis
Determination of MP and Ni (Ⅱ) in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), XRD image (b), XPS spectrum (c) and fluorescent emission spectra (d) of CDs. (e) IR spectra of CDs, CDs@Tb and FCC. (f) UV absorption (green line) and fluorescent spectral (red line) of FCC.

The effects of pH value (a), incubation temperature (b), and the incubation time (c) on the ratiometric detection of MP and Ni (Ⅱ). (d) UV–vis spectrum of p-NP (Gray shadow) and fluorescence spectral of FCC with 0, 40 μM p-NP or 15 μM Ni (Ⅱ).
Fluorescent spectra of FCC with different concentration of MP (a) and Ni (Ⅱ) (c). Linear relationship of MP (b) and Ni (Ⅱ) (d) with FCC.

(a) The scheme of dual-targets detection by ratiometric detection system in co-existing situation of Ni (Ⅱ) and MP. (b) The effect of MP (20 μM) on the detection of Ni (Ⅱ) (4 μM) by using the present method. (c) The effect of Ni (Ⅱ) (4 μM) on the detection of MP (20 μM) by using the present method. (d) The effect of Sm-CeO2 on the fluorescent intensity of FCC.
Selectivity of the FCC against other pesticides (a) and metal ions (b).
The visual detection for MP (a) and Ni (Ⅱ) (c) at different concentration. The linear curves of MP (b) and Ni (Ⅱ) (d) detection by using smartphone.
Graphic abstract of the two-step strategy for dual-mode detection of methyl-paraoxon and Ni (Ⅱ).
Recoveries obtained from the detection of Ni (Ⅱ) in spiked samples (n = 3).
Recoveries obtained from the detection of MP in spiked samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A two-step strategy for simultaneous dual-mode detection of methyl-paraoxon and Ni (Ⅱ)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exogenous pollution of Chinese medicinal materials by pesticide residues and heavy metal ions has attracted great attention. Relying on the rapid development of nanotechnology and multidisciplinary fields, fluorescent techniques have been widely applied in contaminant detection and pollution monitoring due to their advantages of simple preparation, low cost, high throughput and others. Most importantly, synchronous detection of multi-targets has always been pursued as one of the major goals in the design of fluorescent probes. Herein, we firstly develop a simultaneous sensing method for methyl-paraoxon (MP) and Nickel ion (Ni, Ⅱ) by using carbon based fluorescent nanocomposite with ratiometric signal readout and nanozyme. Notably, the designed system showed excellent effectiveness even when the two pollutants co-exist. Under the optimum conditions, this method provides low limits of detection of 1.25 µM for methyl-paraoxon and 0.01 µM for Ni (Ⅱ). To further verify the reliability, recovery studies of these two analytes were performed on ginseng radix et rhizoma, nelumbinis semen, and water samples. In addition, smartphone-based visual analysis has been introduced to expand its applicability in point of care detection. This work not only expands the application of the dual-mode approach to pollutant detection, but also provides insights into the analysis of multiple pollutants in a single assay.

ce:section.ce:para: While human beings have been enjoying the benefits of industrial and agricultural development, we are also the victims who have been suffering from the widespread and severe pollution of heavy metals, pesticide residues and biotoxin caused in the process of production (Chen et al., 2021a; Le et al., 2017; Schulz et al., 2021). As a class of natural plants with distinctive medicinal activities, Chinese medicinal materials also bear the risk of contamination by the above-mentioned pollutants (Cheng et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). Heavy metal ions are difficult to be degraded and easy to accumulate, causing harm to human health. Although some metal ions have proven essential elements for the human body, excessive intake will produce adverse reactions, especially nickel as a long-lasted environmental hazard, which can be released into the environment in large amounts in electroplating and steel manufacturing (Chow et al., 2021; Rizwan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). For pesticides, they have been widely applied in the insects and weeds elimination for increasing crop yield. In particular, organophosphates as the most widely used pesticides like methyl-paraoxon (MP), could directly inhibit the proteins in the human body (e.g., Acetylcholinesterase) and induce the possibility of developing neurological diseases (Lakshmi et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2018a; Zou et al., 2018). These toxic pollutants have formed a vast threat to human health by food chain accumulation (Driessnack et al., 2017; Li et al., 2021). Eventually, many countries and regions have established strict standards to control the limited residue of toxic pollutants in water, food, and other edible portions. (El-Kady and Abdel-Wahhab, 2018; Hass et al., 2011; Li et al., 2020).

In terms of the conventional analytical strategy, pollutant determination has relied on the precision apparatus, including micellar electrokinetic chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and others (Fu et al., 2020; Peeters et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2016). In recent years, new analytical methods involving ultra-violet absorption, electrochemical, colorimetric, Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, fluorescent and visual detection techniques have been frequently reported (Ding et al., 2021; Kong et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2013; Ma et al., 2020; Qiu et al., 2019, 2021; Tümay et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019, 2021; Yang et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2021, 2020). In contrast to the conventional methods, these novel analytical strategies can achieve high sensitivity and selectivity without complicated pre-treatment in a short time. Most importantly, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) as an rapid authoritative technique has been documented in the latest Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition) and employed for the detection of aflatoxin, which may indicate that more and more rapid methods will be selected as standards in the near future (Xu et al., 2021). Among them, fluorescent-based techniques featured with rapid response, high stability, simple operation and short time consumption have attracted many scientists to detect pollutants (Chen et al., 2021b; Dewangan et al., 2022; Sun and Lei, 2017; Walekar et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2018b). Beyond sensitive analysis for single target, synchronous detection of two or multiple targets has always been considered as the big challenge for fluorescent methods.

By far, several recent literatures have reported new fluorescent strategies for the analysis of more than one target with outstanding performances, including simpleness, convenience, cost-effectiveness and so on. The above methods are mainly categorized as two groups, targeting multiple analytes by using single channel (Gao et al., 2018; Wiwasuku et al., 2021) and mutually independent channel (Li et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2018), respectively. Obviously, analytical methods with the usage of different channel/wavelength could reduce the interference from other analytes to some extent. More importantly, the sensitivity and accuracy of the methods could be largely improved by combing multi-channel technique with ratiometric detection mode. Herein, we will report a new fluorescence assay that can simultaneously detect MP and nickel ions. Sensitive detection of different substances could be achieved in two steps by using the prepared fluorescent carbon based composite (FCC) and samarium doped nanoceria as nanozyme. On the one hand, MP and Ni (Ⅱ) could be detected via blue (420 nm) and green (546 nm) fluorescent signal channels, respectively. On the other hand, this work provides a ratiometric detection strategy for two pollutants in co-existing situation. In addition, this fluorescent assay also could be extended to smartphone based colorimetric analysis for different pollutants observed with the naked eye under UV (Ultraviolet) lamp irradiation. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first time to detect MP and Ni (Ⅱ) simultaneously by using single system with fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout pathway. Finally, the proposed method has been successfully applied for targeted detection in two Chinese medicinal materials and water samples.

3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid, 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, nickel chloride hexahydrate, terbium (Ⅲ) chloride, p-nitrophenol were supplied by Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (China). MP, dichlorvos, fonotos, paraoxon-ethyl, parathion-methyl, malaoxon, diazion, monocrotophos, azinphos-methyl, fensulfothion and malathion were supplied by Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Germany). Samarium doped nanoceria (Sm-CeO2) was supplied by Sigma reagent Co., Ltd. (China).

UV absorption spectra, IR (Infrared) spectroscopy and Fluorescence Spectrophotometer were observed by DR 6000 UV–vis (Ultraviolet-visible) spectrophotometer (HACH, USA.), Nicolet iS10 IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.) and Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. Sample images for visual detection were obtained in 96-well plates by ZF-7 ultraviolet analyzer (Jiapeng technology Co., Ltd, China.). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were measured by FEI Talos F200X (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by Bruker D8 advance (Bruker, USA.).

The CDs were synthesized from 3-Aminobenzeneboronic acid through the simple hydrothermal method. Firstly, 10 mg of aminobenzeneboronic acid was well dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and the pH of obtained solution was adjusted to 5. The mixture was heated in a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle at 180 ℃ for 6 h. Secondly, the brown solution was obtained after cooling to room temperature, and 400 μL of 0.5 M NaOH was added for precipitating the unreacted aminobenzeneboronic acid and other impurities. The mixture was centrifuged at 4700 rpm for removing the precipitate to obtain the purified CDs solution. Thirdly, 10 mL of the as-prepared CDs was mixed with 20 mL of 100 μM TbCl3 and 20 mL of 300 μM 2, 6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid in a 150 mL round-bottom flask for 30 min. Finally, the FCC solution with light yellow color was prepared and stored at 4 ℃ for further use.

First of all, Ni (Ⅱ) was detected by using the developed method. Typically, 100 μL of Ni (Ⅱ) contaminated solution was directly mixed with 250 μL of FCC and 650 μL of deionized water for fluorescent detection. Then, the fluorescent intensity was used to calculate the concentration of Ni (Ⅱ) residue. MP was then detected with the pretreatment of Sm-CeO2 as the second step while the fluorescent spectral obtained after the procedure of Ni (Ⅱ) detection was treated as the control group. 2 mL of raw MP contaminated sample was heated with 10 mg Sm-CeO2 nanozyme for 20 min at 65 ℃ water bath. After the reaction, the mixture was centrifuged (4700 rpm, 5 min) and the supernatant was transferred into 2 mL of the centrifuge tube. Subsequently, 100 μL of supernatant was mixed with 250 μL of FCC and 650 μL of deionized water for fluorescent detection.

The proposed method could be applied in the visual detection of MP and Ni (Ⅱ) due to the high fluorescent intensity and multi-emission features under UV lamp irradiation. At first, 50 μL of probe solution was added to the 96-well plate. Then, the sample solution after the preparation procedure was dropped into the 96-well plate and mixed thoroughly with FCC. Color values of the captured images by smartphone under 254 nm UV lamp would be extracted by using the smartphone software “Rgb” for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The procedure of real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability of the method, real water samples were collected from the campus tap and lake. Through centrifuging, the supernatant was filtered by the 0.22 µm microporous membrane. The filtrate was added to the probe solution for Ni (Ⅱ) detection at the first step. And then, MP will be calculated in accordance with the above procedure for fluorescent and visual detection. As for plant samples, ethyl acetate was used and mixed with these samples (2 mL/g). Then the mixture was sonicated in an ice bath for 3 min. The supernatant was centrifugated and filtered by 0.22 µm microporous membrane and placed in nitrogen-blowing apparatus for solvent removal. After reconstituted with deionized water, the sample was finally added and mixed thoroughly with the probe solution for detection.

To obtain the dual-signal channels, the preparation of FCC was through the surface modification of CDs, which were synthesized via the simple hydrothermal method (Luo et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). CDs were scanned by TEM, XRD, XPS, fluorescent spectrometer for characterization. As presented in Fig. 1a, the CDs represented good dispersibility in the water solvent with a near-circular shape, with an average size of 2.8 nm (Fig. S1 , ESI). (Jiao et al., 2018). As shown in the HRTEM image, the distance of lattice spacing was about 0.20 nm. After the calculation, the lattice spacing distance corresponded with the graphene plane (100), which indicated the synthesized CDs possessing a similar structure with graphitic (Zhao et al., 2021). As Fig. 1b. shown, the XRD diffraction (Background subtracted) of CDs showed a characteristic peak around 27°, which matched the graphite carbon (002) (Dong et al., 2014; Edison et al., 2016). XPS analysis was used to characterize the composition of CDs. Fig. 1 c shows that the XPS spectrum of CDs contains a C 1 s peak at 284.00 eV, an N 1 s peak at 398.25 eV, an O 1 s peak at 533.25 eV and a B 1 s peak at 190.50 eV, respectively. After the carbon calibration, the XPS spectra of carbon, nitrogen and boron elements were analyzed for peak fitting. High-resolution of C 1 s spectra (Fig. S2a , ESI) indicated that the C-B (284.30 eV), CC/C-C (284.75 eV), C-O (285.45 eV) and C-N (285.70 eV) bonds were presented in CDs. In Fig. S2b (ESI), XPS analysis result of B 1 s has demonstrated two peaks, B-O (191.70 eV) and C-B (191.00 eV) bonds in the structure (Luo et al., 2020). In addition, 399.30 eV, 400.55 eV and 401.60 eV of N 1 s spectrum could be assigned to C-N-C, N-C and N-H bonds (Fig. S2c , ESI) (Tian et al., 2017). For optical properties, Fig. 1d showed the maximum emission intensity of CDs remained at 420 nm while excitation wavelength changed from 260 to 300 nm, which reveals that the particle size distribution of CDs was concentrated on narrow size with excitation-independent characteristics (Zhao et al., 2019). During the formation of FCC, IR was applied to observe the structural changes. As presented in Fig. 1e, IR spectral changed obviously at around 890, 950, 1464, 3350, 3450 and 3650 cm−1 after terbium (Ⅲ) and pyridinedicarboxylic acid were added into CDs solution, which were contributed to the FCC formation. As displayed in the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1f), its maximum absorption peak was 273 nm. Under the 273 nm excitation, FCC represented four characteristic fluorescent peaks at 494, 546, 586 and 624 nm, which were ascribed to 5D4 → 7F6, 5D4 → 7F5, 5D4 → 7F4, and 5D4 → 7F3 transitions (Yang et al., 2017).

FCC display the representative fluorescent peak at 420 and 546 nm under 273 nm excitation, which were respectively selected for MP and Ni (Ⅱ) quantification. To approach the best fluorescent response, analytical conditions including pH, incubation time and incubation temperature were optimized (
Fig. 2a-c). Finally, the pH 10, 0 min and 298 K were selected as the optimum condition for further experiment. As exhibited in the Scheme 1, the catalytical product p-NP is the essential step for MP detection in this assay. Thus, the usage of Sm-CeO2, reaction temperature and incubation time for the catalytic reaction were optimized. As shown in Fig. S3 (ESI), the highest yield of p-NP was achieved when 10 mg Sm-CeO2 was incubated with the studied MP at 65 °C for 20 min. Under the optimum conditions, the stability and reusability of established FCC system was tested. As exhibited in Fig. S4a (ESI), the FCC detection system showed good stability (RSD<2.00%) for continuous detection of MP and Ni (Ⅱ) within seven days. For reusability study, Sm-CeO2 was initially heated with MP (100 μM) for hydrolysis. After the reaction ended, another new MP sample solution (100 μM) was repeatedly reacted with Sm-CeO2. As the Fig. S4b (ESI) shown, Sm-CeO2 exhibited stable catalytic activity for MP degradation and good recyclability.

Under the mentioned optimum conditions above, the FCC showed the best detection capacity for MP and Ni (Ⅱ). The possible detection mechanism was studied. As shown in Fig. 2d, UV–vis absorption spectrum of p-NP was overlapped with the emission spectrum of FCC. With increasing the p-NP concentration, fluorescent quenching effect and peak shift were caused due to the above partial spectral overlap ( Fig. 3a). Besides, the lifetimes of FCC without/with 80 μM p-NP were 1.92 and 1.97 ns, which have no significant difference (Fig. S5 , ESI). All the above results show there is no energy transfer between FCC and p-NP, which implies the potential detection mechanism for MP detection might be the inner filter effect. In terms of Ni (Ⅱ) detection, it was reported Ni (Ⅱ) represent high affinity to N-doped carbon-based probe and lead to fluorescent quenching due to the interaction of the N-based functional group and Ni (Ⅱ) (Gong and Liang, 2019). In this work, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method and showed high stability in the presence of Ni (Ⅱ) at high concentrations. During FCC formation, terbium ions would link CDs and pyridinedicarboxylic acid with the carboxyl and amino group. After Ni (Ⅱ) mixed with FCC, Ni (Ⅱ) might rapidly automatically replace terbium (Ⅲ) (Gallardo et al., 2017).

Organophosphate and heavy metal are becoming a serious environmental crisis which could affect the food safety of human beings. FCC could sensitively and specifically detect MP and Ni (Ⅱ). After the detection system was established, the quenching rate was used to calculate the concentration of MP and Ni (Ⅱ). The QR of MP or Ni (Ⅱ) was calculated by the following Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1)
Q R = S
I 420
I 546
/ B
I 420
I 546
(2)
Q R = S
I 546
I 420
/ B
I 546
I 420

I420 and I546 were the fluorescent intensity of FCC at 420 and 546 nm, while S and B are the samples in the presence and absence of MP or Ni (Ⅱ). As shown in Fig. 3, the fluorescent quenching degree presented a good linear relationship with the concentrations of two analytes. As shown in Fig. 3a and c, the ratiometric detection system could maintain the reference signals in both MP and Ni (Ⅱ) detection. The fluorescence was gradually quenched with the concentration of MP increased from 1.25 to 60 μM. QR1 represented high linear trends at two linear ranges. Moreover, fluorescence was quenched obviously together with red-shifted from 420 to 430 nm when p-NP absorption increased, which is in accordance with the quenching feature of inner filter effect. As Fig. 3b shown, the first linearity (Y=−0.01053*X + 0.9865, R2 =0.9968) from 1.25 to 20 μM was noticed, and the second linear range was from 20 to 60 μM with the linearity (Y=−0.005625 *X + 0.8908, R2 =0.9914). The limit of detection (LOD) for MP was 1.25 μM, based on S/N > 3 (where N is the standard deviation). As shown in Fig. 3c and d, this present detection systems also exhibited a sensitive capacity for Ni (Ⅱ) detection, while LOD was 0.01 μM with good linearity (Y=−0.07302*X + 0.9390, R2 =0.9926) from 0.1 to 8 μM. Compared with the other methods, this ratiometric detection system showed high or comparable performances for pollutant analysis (Table S1 , ESI).

Notably, this work was designed for MP and Ni (Ⅱ) detection even in their co-existing situations. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, Ni (Ⅱ) was first detected and quantified in the co-existing situation. Then, the working solution was treated as a reference sample for further MP quantification. After being catalyzed by Sm-nanoceria, the working solution would be analyzed for accurate determination of MP. The effectiveness of this strategy was confirmed and shown in Fig. 4. Even in raw MP contaminated samples, the ratiometric probe could be sensitively quenched by Ni (Ⅱ) (Fig. 4b). And the QR calculated by Eq. (2) showed a negligible difference from the single Ni (Ⅱ) group. The impact of Ni (Ⅱ) on MP detection was also verified. The present system was used for MP detection in the sample co-existing with Ni (Ⅱ). After pre-treatment with Sm-CeO2, MP could also be detected while the water sample contaminated with Ni (Ⅱ) was treated as blank or reference sample for calculation. The QR1 is highly similar to that of MP only group (Fig. 4). Hence, this has been proof of the successful construction of this dual-targets detection strategy. Furthermore, nanozyme impact was studied. As shown in Fig. 4d, Sm-CeO2 treatment process hardly affected this detection system. In general, the whole strategy was successfully established without mutual interference. The ratiometric system could be used for qualitative and quantitative detection of dual-targets even in co-existing situations.

Selectivity was tested by using different kinds of pesticides and metal ions. As presented in Fig. 5, the FCC represented a good selectivity for MP and Ni (Ⅱ). On the one hand, the result indicated other pesticides (fonotos, malaoxon, diazion, dichlorvos, malathion, monocrotophos, parathion-methyl, paraoxon-ethyl, azinphos-methyl and fensulfothion) at same concentration could not be detected (Fig. 5a). On the other hand, the QRs of K (Ⅰ), Zn (Ⅱ), Cr (Ⅱ), Sb (Ⅲ), Mn (Ⅱ), Ag (Ⅰ), Au (Ⅲ), Na (Ⅰ) represented negligible changes compared with sample group (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MP and Ni (Ⅱ) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to test the feasibility of this developed method, tap water, lake water, Ginseng Radix and Nelumbinis Semen were utilized. Water and food samples were collected from the University of Macau and the wet market in Macau, respectively. As presented in
Tables 1 and 2, recoveries of Ni (Ⅱ) and MP were from 89.73% ― 109.00% and 86.00% ― 104.96%, respectively. GC-MS and ICP-MS were also applied and verified the results. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S2 (ESI), instrument-based methods could not detect the signal of the pollutant in the water and plant samples, which is consistent with our result.

Visual detection test was then investigated under the 254 nm UV lamp irradiation. MP and Ni (Ⅱ) were detected via different fluorescent signals, which were beneficial for visual detection of dual-targets. Relying on this system, MP at different concentrations exhibited various green colour in the plate after being catalyzed by Sm-nanoceria ( Fig. 6a). Along with Ni (Ⅱ) concentration decreased, FCC displayed the blue colour from deep to shallow (Fig. 6c). In addition, mobile software "Rgb" was applied to extract colour values of the sample in order to develop colorimetric assay for detection. As displayed in Fig. 6b and d, the linear curves of MP and Ni (Ⅱ) were Log(CMP)= −50.61X+ 175.7 and Log(CNi)= −31.04X+ 133.9, respectively. The linear ranges of the present colorimetric methods were 14.3–285 μM for MP and 2.85–285 μM for Ni (Ⅱ). Apparently, the present visual detection method could display the results with simple preparation, high sensitivity, and wide linear range for quantification of dual-targets.

A ratiometric detection system for MP and Ni (Ⅱ) was designed by using carbon-based composite in two steps. The established system demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity for dual-targets. Even in the co-existing condition of MP and Ni (Ⅱ), the as-designed probe could detect both two pollutants. Even in the natural plants and water samples, the FCC showed the good recoveries in complex conditions. Furthermore, due to the short sample preparation time, good reusability and simple operation, the developed method demonstrated its potential as a promising candidate for monitoring MP and Ni (Ⅱ) in real samples. The main contribution of this work is not only to develop a new analytical platform with desirable performance, but also to inspire the design and development of fluorescent probes for food safety and environmental monitoring of multiple analytes.

Mai Luo: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ling Chen: Methodology, Visualization. Jinchao Wei: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Xiping Cui: Data curation. Zehua Cheng: Resources. Ting Wang: Formal analysis. Incheng Chao: Investigation. Yunyang Zhao: Investigation. Hao Gao: Supervision. Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review& editing. Peng Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing − review& editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Macau Science and Technology Development Fund (147/2019/A3 and 0047/2020/AGJ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (81903794), Guangdong International Science and Technology Cooperation Base (2021A0505020015), Innovative and Research Teams Project of Guangdong Higher Education Institution (2021KCXTD001), Guangxi Innovation-driven Development Special Foundation Project (GuiKe AA18118049) and the Research Committee of the University of Macau (MYRG2018-00239-ICMS).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2022.113668.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
